verses.commentary: Therefore: because you are born again (1:23), rid yourselves (apotithēmi, ἀποτίθημι): put off like dirty clothes. Five relational sins listed: malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, slander. New birth demands new behavior. What defined the old life must be discarded.

Like newborn babies, crave (epipotheō, ἐπιποθέω) pure spiritual milk. Desire for God's word should be as intense as an infant's hunger. Growth requires nourishment. Salvation has begun; maturity requires feeding on truth.

Now that you have tasted (Psalm 34:8): experiential knowledge, not theory. The Lord is good (chrēstos, χρηστός): kind, gracious. Tasting leads to craving. Those who experience God's goodness hunger for more.

Come to Him, the living Stone. Christ is the cornerstone, rejected by humans but chosen and precious to God. Living Stone: not dead religion but living relationship. Rejection by the world does not negate God's choice.

Believers are living stones, built into a spiritual house. Corporate imagery: individual stones form one building. A holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices acceptable through Christ. All believers are priests; Christ is mediator. No human intermediary needed.

Isaiah 28:16 quoted: God lays a cornerstone in Zion, chosen and precious. The one who trusts will never be put to shame (kataischynō, καταισχύνω): disgraced, disappointed. Faith in Christ is secure foundation. Trust will not be betrayed.

To believers, the stone is precious (timē, τιμή): honored, valued. To unbelievers, the stone builders rejected has become the cornerstone (Psalm 118:22). The world's rejection becomes God's exaltation. Human assessment is reversed by divine purpose.

To unbelievers, Christ is a stone that causes stumbling (Isaiah 8:14). They stumble because they disobey the message. Appointed to this (eis ho kai etethēsan, εἰς ὃ καὶ ἐτέθησαν): their destiny is tied to their disobedience. God's sovereignty includes human rebellion's consequences.

But you: stark contrast. Chosen people, royal priesthood, holy nation, God's special possession (Exodus 19:5-6). Identity defined by God's choosing, not human achievement. Purpose: declare the praises of Him who called you from darkness to light. Salvation is for witness.

Hosea 1:9-10, 2:23 applied to Gentile believers. Once not a people, now the people of God. Once without mercy, now recipients of mercy. Identity transformation: from nobodies to God's treasured possession. Grace creates a people out of nothing.

Dear friends: affection and urgency. As foreigners and exiles (paroikos kai parepidēmos, πάροικος καὶ παρεπίδημος): temporary residents, not permanent citizens. Abstain from sinful desires that wage war (strateuomai, στρατεύομαι) against your soul. Sin is military conflict, not minor struggle.

Live good lives among pagans. They accuse you of wrongdoing (kakopoios, κακοποιός), but good deeds will be undeniable. They may glorify God on the day He visits: either at conversion or final judgment. Witness is visible, not merely verbal.

Submit (hypotassō, ὑποτάσσω) to human authority for the Lord's sake. Not because government is always right, but because submission reflects trust in God's sovereignty. Christian obedience is theologically motivated, not merely pragmatic.

Governors sent to punish wrongdoers and commend those who do right. Government's purpose: restrain evil, promote good (Romans 13:1-7). When it functions rightly, believers submit. When it demands sin, believers obey God (Acts 5:29).

God's will: doing good silences ignorant talk of foolish people. Slander is refuted by righteous living, not arguments. Consistent obedience shames false accusations. Character is the most powerful apologetic.

Live as free people, but do not use freedom as cover for evil. Christian liberty is not license. Freedom is found in serving God (douloi theou, δοῦλοι θεοῦ), not self. Paradox: true freedom is slavery to Christ.

Four concise commands: respect everyone, love the family of believers, fear God, honor the emperor. Universal respect, special love for believers, reverent awe for God, submission to authority. Layered loyalties, properly ordered.

Slaves (oiketēs, οἰκέτης): household servants. Submit in reverent fear, not only to good masters but also harsh ones. Christian submission transcends circumstance. Obedience is rendered to God, not merely to human authority.

Bearing unjust suffering while conscious of God is commendable (charis, χάρις): grace, favor. Endurance for God's sake honors Him. Awareness of God's presence transforms suffering from meaningless to purposeful.

No credit for enduring punishment you deserve. But suffering for doing good and enduring it is commendable before God. The nature of suffering matters. Persecution for righteousness is unique and honored.

To this you were called: suffering for righteousness is part of the Christian calling. Christ suffered, leaving an example (hypogrammos, ὑπογραμμός): a model to trace. Follow in His steps (ichnos, ἴχνος): footprints. Imitation of Christ includes His suffering.
Isaiah 53:9 quoted. Christ committed no sin, no deceit in His mouth. He is the sinless substitute. His innocence qualifies Him to bear others' guilt. Perfect obedience made Him the perfect sacrifice.

When insulted, He did not retaliate. When suffering, He made no threats. Instead, He entrusted Himself to God who judges justly. Christ's response to injustice: trust the Father. Vengeance belongs to God (Romans 12:19).

Isaiah 53:4-5, 12 echoed. Christ bore our sins in His body on the tree. Substitutionary atonement: He took what we deserve. We died to sins, now live for righteousness. By His wounds you have been healed (iaomai, ἰάομαι): spiritual healing, forgiveness.

Isaiah 53:6 applied. You were like sheep going astray, now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer (episkopos, ἐπίσκοπος) of your souls. Conversion is return to Christ, the Good Shepherd. He guards, guides, and owns His flock.
